11: The place-name pereṣ ʿuzzāh (‘Breach of Uzzah’) that David gives to the threshing floor of Chidon encodes a double irony. The name ʿuzzāh derives from the root ʿōz (strength, might) — the man’s name literally means ‘YHWH’s strength’ or simply ‘strength.’ Uzzah is killed at the moment of deploying that strength: reaching out (wayyišlaḥ yādô) to steady the Ark. The verb pāraṣ (to breach, break out, burst forth) — wayyippeṣ bô ʾelōhîm, ‘God broke out against him’ — names the violent, boundary-crossing force of divine holiness when it encounters unauthorized contact. The pereṣ (breach) that kills Uzzah is the Ark asserting its own holiness against human management. The same verb pāraṣ appears in 1 Chronicles 14:11 (YHWH breaks through David’s enemies = Baal-perazim) and 1 Chronicles 15:13 (David’s explanation of the Uzzah incident: pāraṣ bānû YHWH, ‘YHWH broke out against us’). The Chronicler deliberately uses this root across all three chapters to create a theological signature: pāraṣ is YHWH’s characteristic verb — he breaks boundaries, whether against enemies (1 Chronicles 14:11) or against unauthorized approach (1 Chronicles 13:11). The Ark-cart episode is not merely a liturgical error; it is a failure to reckon with the pāraṣ-character of YHWH’s holiness.
1: David’s consultation with the commanders before any action is the Chronicler’s signature editorial addition to the Samuel source (2 Samuel 6:1 simply says David gathered thirty thousand). The deliberative assembly — leaders of thousands and hundreds, then “every leader” (kol nāśîʾ) — frames the ark-retrieval as a national covenantal act requiring unanimous consent, not a unilateral royal initiative. The Chronicler consistently portrays legitimate Davidic action as communally ratified.
2: David’s speech to the assembly introduces the conditional double-clause: ʾim ʿālêḵem ṭôḇ ûmin-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû (‘if it seems good to you, and if it is of the LORD our God’). Both human assent and divine warrant are required. The formulation reflects the Chronicler’s political theology: legitimate religious action needs both popular consensus and covenantal authorization. David’s appeal to “our brethren remaining in all the territories of Israel” is another Chronicler addition, emphasizing the all-Israel character of the project.
3: The charge to retrieve the ark is grounded in a critique of Saul’s reign: “for we did not inquire of it (lōʾ ḏᵉrašnûhû) during Saul’s days.” The verb dārash (to seek, inquire of) is the Chronicler’s most characteristic theological verb: seeking YHWH through his appointed means (ark, temple, prayer) is the mark of a faithful king, and its absence is the diagnostic of Saul’s failure. The Chronicler will return to this failure explicitly at Saul’s death (1 Chronicles 10:13–14: “Saul died because he was unfaithful to the LORD… and he did not inquire (dāraš) of the LORD”).
4: “All the assembly agreed to do so, for the thing seemed right to all the people.” The unanimous affirmation (“all” appearing twice) is the Chronicler’s signal of an approved covenantal act. Compare the Solomonic temple dedication (2 Chronicles 5:3, 6; 1 Chronicles 7:8) where “all Israel” similarly ratifies the sanctuary act. Unanimous assemblies in Chronicles mark moments when human decision aligns with divine will.
5: David assembles Israel from Shihor of Egypt to the entrance of Hamath — the ideal geographical extent of the land of promise (Numbers 34:5, 8; Joshua 13:3; 1 Kings 8:65). The Chronicler is using the outer boundary formula to signal that the ark-retrieval is a defining national moment requiring the full breadth of covenant Israel. Samuel’s parallel simply says “thirty thousand chosen men of Israel.” The Chronicler’s scope is theological, not military.
6: Baalah is given as an alternative name for Kiriath-jearim (‘city of forests’). The Baalah name (from baʿal, ‘lord/mistress’; feminine form = ‘the mistress’) reflects the site’s Canaanite prehistory; Joshua 15:9–10 uses both names interchangeably. The ark had been at Kiriath-jearim for twenty years (1 Samuel 7:2), hosted in the house of Abinadab. The destination is named in full: “to bring up from there the ark of God, the LORD who is enthroned above the cherubim, upon whom his name is called.” The throne-above-cherubim formula (cf. 1 Samuel 4:4; 2 Samuel 6:2; Psalms 80:1) is the ark’s highest theological description.
7: The fatal error: the ark is placed on a “new cart” (ʿăgālāh ḥᵃḏāšāh). This was the Philistine method of returning the ark after their capture (1 Samuel 6:7–8), not the Mosaic ordinance. Numbers 7:9 specifies that the Kohathite Levites must carry the most holy things “on their shoulders” (bakkātēp); the ark was explicitly excluded from ox-cart transport. David adopts the Philistine technological solution rather than the Mosaic liturgical ordinance, and the consequences follow immediately. Ch15:13–15 (already in the commentary) explains the principle David eventually articulates.
8: The celebratory procession with “all their might” (bᵉḵol ʿōz) and the full ensemble of instruments — songs, harps (kinnōrôt), lyres (nᵉḇālîm), tambourines (tuppîm), cymbals (mṣiltayim), and trumpets (ḥăṣōṣᵉrôt) — is the Chronicler’s expanded version of Samuel’s similar but shorter list. The musical catalogue anticipates the full Levitical orchestra that will be formally organized in 1 Chronicles 15–16 for the successful ark-transport. The exuberant worship before the ark is not wrong; the method of carrying is.
9: The threshing floor of Chidon (kîḏôn) is named Nachon’s threshing floor in the Samuel parallel (2 Samuel 6:6). The variant may reflect different regional traditions or textual transmission. The verb for the oxen’s stumbling (šāmaṭ, ‘to let fall/shake loose’) is rare; it occurs only here and in 2 Samuel 6:6. Uzzah’s response is instinctive: he “reached out his hand” (wayyišlaḥ yadô) to steady the ark — the same verb used for the forbidden extending of hands toward holy objects (Exodus 19:13; cf. 2 Samuel 6:6–7).
10: YHWH’s anger kindles against Uzzah (wayyiḥar ʾap YHWH bᵉʿuzzāh) and he strikes him down. The narrative offers the explanation “because he put his hand to the ark” — but the deeper cause, which David later articulates (1 Chronicles 15:13), is the improper mode of transport. The immediate cause is Uzzah’s touch; the structural cause is the violated ordinance. The tension between these two levels of causation is part of what makes the episode theologically difficult: Uzzah’s instinct was protective, but it violated the boundary. The holiness of the ark was not suspended by good intentions.
12: David’s fear (wayyîrāʾ dāwîḏ ʾet hāʾĕlōhîm bayyôm hahûʾ) marks the shift from anger (v11) to reverent awe. The fear of God (yirʾat ʾĕlōhîm) is the appropriate response to the holiness-encounter; anger is the first human response, fear the subsequent and correct one. The question “How can I bring the ark of God to me?” (êḵ ʾāḇîʾ ʾēlî ʾēt ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm) is not answered here but in the entire procedural preparation of 1 Chronicles 15. The question is not rhetorical; it demands a cultic answer.
13: David diverts the ark to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite (gittî, ‘from Gath’). The name Obed-edom means ‘servant of Edom’ or possibly ‘worshipper of Edom’; the gittî epithet marks him as connected to Gath (a Philistine city), making him either a Levite from Gath-rimmon (a Levitical city, Joshua 21:24) or a Gittite who had joined Israel (like Ittai the Gittite in 2 Samuel 15:19). The Chronicler later identifies Obed-edom as a Korahite Levite (1 Chronicles 15:18, 21; 1 Chronicles 16:38), resolving the ambiguity in favor of Levitical qualification.
14: The ark remains three months with Obed-edom, and “the LORD blessed the household (bêt) of Obed-edom and all he had.” The blessing formula echoes the Abrahamic covenant-language (Genesis 12:3; 1 Chronicles 22:17); the ark functions as a portable locus of Abrahamic blessing. The three-month period is significant: in Levitical liturgical practice, three months marks the completion of a quarter; the period of blessing serves as evidence that the ark is not intrinsically dangerous but becomes so only when handled in violation of its ordinances. This blessing motivates David’s decision to complete the transfer (14:1 and following, then 1 Chronicles 15).
